lines.text: Section four. DNA sequencing.
If genome sequences are the data, DNA sequencing is the machinery that produces them.
And the history of DNA sequencing is essentially the history of an exponential curve in cost and throughput.
The original sequencing technology was Sanger sequencing, named for Frederick Sanger, who developed the method in the 1970s.
Sanger sequencing reads up to about a thousand base pairs of DNA at a time, using gel electrophoresis and fluorescent dye chemistry to determine the base at each position.
Early genome projects relied entirely on Sanger sequencing, which limited them severely — a thousand bases per read is not very much when you are trying to sequence a multi-million-base genome.
The strategy that opened up large-scale genomes was called shotgun sequencing, championed in the 1990s by J. Craig Venter and others.
The idea is conceptually simple.
Take a large piece of DNA — say, an entire chromosome — and chop it into many smaller fragments, randomly.
Sequence each fragment with Sanger sequencing in parallel.
Then use computer algorithms to find overlaps between the fragments and assemble them back into the full sequence, in silico.

This works because, if you fragment the original DNA into enough pieces and sequence each piece multiple times, the overlaps unambiguously tell you the full sequence — provided your assembly algorithm can handle the combinatorics.
Shotgun sequencing enabled bacterial genomes to be sequenced quickly and affordably, and it remains the dominant strategy for sequencing chromosome-sized DNA today.
The hardware kept improving.
Gel electrophoresis gave way to capillary electrophoresis for sequencing of individual cloned DNA pieces — common in synthetic biology when you need to verify a new construct.
And for genome-scale work, the chemistry has moved on entirely.

Second-generation sequencing — Illumina being the dominant example — and third-generation sequencing — PacBio, Oxford Nanopore — sequence short DNA fragments in massive parallel, producing data sets in the terabyte range and relying heavily on computational pipelines to assemble the reads.
For synthetic biology, fast and cheap DNA sequencing is a prerequisite for almost everything.
Every new construct needs to be sequence-verified before you trust it.
Every cloned plasmid, every PCR product, every engineered chassis needs to be sequenced to confirm that the DNA you have is the DNA you designed.
And in projects where the engineered strains will grow and evolve over time — which is most projects — periodic sequencing catches mutations that would otherwise silently break your design.
The Carlson Curve, which appears in this chapter as Figure 3.2, shows DNA sequencing and synthesis costs over the last two decades on a log scale.
A straight line on a log plot is an exponential, and the curve has been roughly exponential for years — sequencing costs have fallen far faster than Moore's Law for transistors.
That cost curve is what made synthetic biology, with its dependence on constant sequencing verification, economically practical.
